title: Time for a Pelvic Health Checkup?
nudge: A routine gynaecology checkup can catch fibroids, ovarian cysts, and early signs of pelvic inflammatory disease — most of which cause no symptoms until they've been present for a while. If you haven't had one in the last year, it's a good time to book. An ultrasound scan is the standard first step and is non-invasive.
action: Book a routine gynaecology checkup with your GP or polyclinic.

title: Check In on Your Ovarian Health
nudge: Ovarian cysts and benign growths are common and most resolve on their own — but some need monitoring to make sure they do. A pelvic ultrasound during a routine checkup is all it takes to get a clear picture. You don't need symptoms to ask for one — it's just good maintenance.
action: Ask for a pelvic ultrasound at your next routine checkup.

title: Know the Signs of Premature Ovarian Insufficiency
who_sees_this: Users aged 18–39, especially those with irregular cycles or flagged conditions
nudge: Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is when the ovaries stop functioning normally before age 40. It affects roughly 1 in 100 women and is often mistaken for stress or lifestyle changes. Signs to watch for include irregular or absent periods, hot flushes, and night sweats. If any of these sound familiar, it's worth getting checked — a simple blood test measuring FSH and estrogen levels is all your GP needs to investigate.
action: If your periods have become irregular or stopped and you're under 40, raise it with your GP and ask about FSH and estrogen testing.

title: Your Weight and Your Hormones Are Connected
nudge: Both significantly low and significantly high body weight can disrupt the hormonal signals that regulate your cycle. This isn't about appearance — it's a clinical connection between metabolic health and hormonal function. If your periods have become irregular alongside noticeable weight changes in either direction, it's worth mentioning at your next GP visit.
action: If your cycle has changed alongside weight changes, mention both to your GP at your next visit.

title: Have You Had Your Annual STI Screen?
nudge: Chlamydia — the most common bacterial STI — causes no symptoms in most people, which is exactly why it goes undetected. Left untreated, it can silently scar the fallopian tubes over time. Annual screening if you're sexually active is quick, confidential, and takes minutes. You don't need to have symptoms or concerns to get tested — it's just a routine part of looking after yourself.
action: Book an STI screen at your nearest polyclinic or DSC Clinic in Singapore. No referral needed.

title: Fallopian Tube Screening — What to Know
who_sees_this: Users who flagged endometriosis, PID history, or irregular cycles; age 25+
nudge: Given your health history, your doctor may at some point recommend checking whether your fallopian tubes are clear. Blockages often cause no symptoms and are only found during investigation. Two procedures are commonly used for this — it's good to know what they involve so you feel informed if it's ever raised.

procedures.name: HyCoSy (Hysterosalpingo-Contrast Ultrasonography)
procedures.description: A small amount of fluid is injected through the cervix and ultrasound tracks how it moves through the fallopian tubes. Blockages show up as interruptions in the flow. It is an outpatient procedure — no surgery, no general anaesthetic.

procedures.name: HSG — Hysterosalpingography (X-ray)
procedures.description: A contrast dye is used instead of fluid and an X-ray tracks its movement through the tubes. Similar purpose to HyCoSy, slightly different method. Also done as an outpatient.

disclaimer: These are investigative scans, not routine ones. Your doctor will recommend them if there's a specific clinical reason — you don't need to request them proactively.
action: If your doctor has mentioned tube screening, ask which procedure they recommend and what to expect on the day.

title: What Is a Laparoscopy — and Should You Know About It?
who_sees_this: Users who flagged endometriosis or PID history; age 22+
nudge: If you've been living with endometriosis symptoms or have a history of pelvic inflammatory disease, a laparoscopy may come up in conversation with your doctor at some point. It's a minor keyhole surgery — a small camera inserted near the navel — that lets doctors examine your womb, fallopian tubes, and ovaries directly. It's the only way to definitively confirm endometriosis, and it's typically day surgery under general anaesthetic. Knowing what it is means you can have a more informed conversation with your doctor if it's ever recommended.
disclaimer: A laparoscopy is not a routine screen. It's recommended when symptoms are strongly consistent with endometriosis or when scans suggest a specific problem. If your GP has raised it, it means they have a clear clinical reason.
action: If a laparoscopy has been mentioned by your doctor, ask them specifically what they expect to find and what the next steps would be based on the results.
